Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees And Expenses; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; **Court Reporter Fees Paid**
Off Calendar Feb-15-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF IDEMITSU RENEWABLES AMERICA INC., PIONEER SOLAR (CO) LLC's Motion For Attorneys Fees And Expenses.

Plaintiff's motion for attorney fees is granted in part. Defendants do not dispute that an award is due, but say the claimed amount is unreasonable.

Rates. Plaintiff's counsel's hourly rates are high for what plaintiff itself has described as a straightforward breach of contract case. On the other hand, the judgment was $10.5 million and defense counsel does not reveal his own hourly rate or suggest what rates should be awarded. Based on 36 years' experience with legal rates in our community, I award $900/hour for the partner and $450/hour for the associate. (See Serrano v. Priest (1977) 20 Cal.3d 25, 49 ("experienced trial judge is the best judge of professional services rendered in his court").)

Staffing. Plaintiff's counsel's staffing of one partner and one associate was appropriate, as were the amount of hours worked. Had plaintiff added another lawyer, defendants would no doubt have claimed over-staffing.
Confidentiality. Plaintiff's litigation of confidentiality issues was appropriate and compensable.
Pioneer. Pioneer Solar was added as an alternative plaintiff due to defendants' arguments. This was part of plaintiff's prosecution of its case.

E-Discovery. The work of e-discovery project managers is compensable.
Block Billing. Block billing is "not objectional per se," and I know how much time legal tasks take without needing granular parsing. (Christian Research Institute v. Alnor (2008) 165 Cal.App.4th 1315, 1325; Serrano, 20 Cal.3d at 49.)

Resolution: Plaintiff is awarded $333,361 in attorney fees. No award is made for amounts asserted in reply papers (to which defendants could not respond).

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

Plaintiff Idemitsu Renewables America, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Prejudgment Interest; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Dec-15-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS IDEMITSU RENEWABLES AMERICA INC. AND PIONEER SOLAR (CO) LLC's Motion For Prejudgment Interest.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for prejudgment interest pursuant to Civil Code sec. 3287(a) is granted. (See North Oakland Medical Clinic v. Rogers (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 824, 830-831 [motion for prejudgment interest may be made "in the form of a motion for new trial no later than the time allowed for filing such a motion"].) Plaintiff was entitled to the $9,523,465 deposit and $968,715 in delay liquidated damages on November 30, 2020, when it made its demand. (See Supervalu, Inc. v. Wexford Underwriting Managers, Inc. (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [prejudgment interest awarded "from the date of demand" for payment].) Those sums are certain. Defendant's dispute related to liability, e.g, whether plaintiff properly terminated the agreement. A dispute over liability, however, does not render the sum due uncertain. (See Olson v. Cory (1983) 35 Cal.3d 390, 402; Howard v. American National Fire Ins. Co. (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 498, 535-536.) There is no double recovery. Plaintiff is therefore entitled to prejudgment interest of $2,874.57 per day from November 30, 2020 up to the date of judgment of October 28, 2021. (Civil Code sec. 3289(b).) The prejudgment interest totals $954,357.24.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Confidential Documents Filed By Plaintiffs In Support Of Their Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Sep-08-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Jovanna Bubar, Esq. (213.892.5364; JBubar@mofo.com) of Morrison & Foerster LLP for Plaintiffs.
Anum Amin, Esq. (213.972.4582; aamin@foley.com) of Foley & Lardner LLP for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS GCL SYSTEM INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY LLC and ZHANG JIAGANG GCL SYSTEM INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY'S Motion To Seal Confidential Documents Filed By Plaintiffs In Support Of Their Motion For Summary Judgment:

Defendants' motion to seal confidential documents filed by plaintiffs in support of their motion for summary judgment is denied in part and granted in part. That Defendants designated (or did not designate) the materials in question as confidential under the parties' stipulated protective order is insufficient for the Court to find whether the requirements of Rules 2.550 and 2.551 are met. (Cal. R. Ct. 2.551(a) ["The court must not permit a record to be filed under seal based solely on the agreement or stipulation of the parties"]; Universal City Studios, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 110 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1282 [rejecting contention that sealing of court documents is permitted "merely upon the agreement of the parties without a specific showing of serious injury"]; see also McNair v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn. (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 25, 36 ["the mere agreement of the parties alone is insufficient to constitute an overriding interest to justify sealing the documents"]; Huffy Corp. v. Superior Court (2003) 112 Cal.App.4th 97, 106-107 [same, declining to seal confidential settlement agreement with nonparty insurer].) Defendants do not satisfactorily explain why Exhibits A, C, G, and H to the Vandenberg Declaration should be sealed, given that they were filed and discussed publicly in previous pleadings, and Defendants did not timely object or move to seal them. (See, e.g., Plaintiffs' First Amended Complaint (filed Mar. 3, 2021), Exs. A, B.) However, Defendants make a sufficient showing that the remaining documents contain sensitive business information, the disclosure of which would cause them economic and commercial harm. (Sun Decl. 5-7.) As to those documents, the Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest.

Order is signed.

The matter is called. ARGUMENT is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendants' demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled. The facts and law as to which plaintiff (or both) holds the claims can be developed in the litigation; this is merely the pleading stage.
